title: Protecting Military Parental Leave Evaluations Act
short_title: Protecting Military Parental Leave Evaluations Act
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.
plain_summary: This bill tells the Department of Defense to make new rules about parental leave for people in the military. The rules will protect military parents from getting bad performance reviews if they take more than 31 days of parental leave in a row. The bill also makes it easier for military parents to use their leave time within two years of having a baby, adopting a child, or getting custody of a child without needing special permission. These changes will help military families take care of their children without hurting their careers.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Protecting Military Parental Leave Evaluations Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Improved Parental Leave for Members of the Armed Forces
sections.text: Congress has learned several important facts about military parental leave. In December two thousand twenty-two, Congress gave military members twelve weeks of paid time off when they have or adopt a baby. This leave can be used during the twelve months after a child is born or adopted, or when a child is placed with them for adoption or long-term foster care. However, there are concerns that military members who take this leave might get worse performance reviews than those who do not take leave. Right now, there is no rule that protects members from getting evaluated during parental leave. This might make eligible members afraid to take the leave they deserve. Each branch of the military has been given power to make leave policies, but they have not given clear instructions about how to handle parental leave when doing performance reviews. All military branches have special codes for times when a member's performance cannot be observed, but these codes are different across branches and do not specifically cover parental leave. Military parents face special challenges, and the twelve weeks of paid family leave for both mothers and fathers is meant to help balance caring for their children with their military duties. Too much paperwork is required to use the twelve weeks of leave beyond one year, which creates extra burden for members and reduces flexibility. Within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, the Secretary of Defense must do two things. First, the Secretary must create rules that protect military members from performance evaluations when they take parental leave that lasts more than thirty-one days in a row. Second, the Secretary must create rules that allow members to take this leave during the two-year period after their child is born, adopted, or placed with them without having to ask for special permission from their military branch leader. The Secretary must also send a report to the Armed Services Committees in both the Senate and House of Representatives explaining how this section has been put into action.
